name: The Johns Hopkins Hullabaloo
description: The Johns Hopkins University Baltimore 1876 Edited by Class of 1917

name: Cecil Hopkins Bagley
motto: "Who falls from what he knows of bliss; Cares little into what abyss." —Byron.
description: One of the striking characteristics of this genial individual is his desire for adventure at any cost. His career thus far has been marked by several close calls, but each time he comes through with the grin that won't come off. "Bags" will allow nothing to stand as a barrier between him and a good dance and the greater the distance to the scene of action, the greater the attraction. At the Glenwood Country Club he is forced to fight 'em off, while in the vicinity of Ellicott City his acquaintance is "big and strong." He holds the same distinction among his class-mates, but the topic of discussion is of a somewhat different variety since co-eds are not a part of us.

name: Baker, Lawrence Henry
clubs: Member Committee of Arrangements, Interclass Debate 1916, 1917
Member Committee for conferring of Tocqueville Medal
description: Baker's idea of happiness is to possess a little house of his own, a cottage preferably, where he may spend his time in the continuous study of the classics. Greek has become the absorbing interest of his life. It is his ambition to become a great scholar, the Erasmus of his generation, and from present prospects there is considerable likelihood of his ambition being realized. While his fellow students were profitably employed in pitching pennies, Baker might have been seen in the library, busily engaged in perusing the tragedies of Aeschylus. On he would read, as in a dream, the pale locks of his hair surmounting a pile of books being the only indication of his whereabouts. He is fearfully efficient, appallingly punctual. He has acquired the habit of always securing "ten" in his studies. He is never late, never absent. Rules and regulations have no terror for him. He could not violate one if he tried. Baker's eyes are now turned toward the south. Gradually it is coming to be realized that his Hellenic soul is susceptible to other attraction than the beauty that lies in the literature of a dead language. Go to it fellow, and here's luck.

name: Baker, Henry Scott
motto: "He was long and slim and lean of limb, But strong as a stripling bear." —Service.
clubs: Varsity Lacrosse Team So., J., S.
1st Lieutenant, R. O. T. C. S.
Interclass Championship Basketball Team, J., S.
description: Henry is probably the most efficient member of the class of 1917. He has gotten more done and higher marks for the energy expended than any other of the engineers. Also, he is popular! More than that,— he is darn popular, and is a leading or driving force in class activities, depending on which one of these powers is needed to produce results. A couple of years ago Henry decided he'd like to play lacrosse, and so he did it. Of course he made the team. Making things and opportunities seems to have become a habit with this Scandinavian. But he doesn't hate himself, even if Doc Whitehead does have doubts on that score. As a senior, he has rendered invaluable service to his class in the capacity of assistant business manager of this book, and was also elected president of the Engineering Society, although he was forced to resign from this position because of other duties. He has been a great success here at Hopkins, and will be one in the world. The class is proud of him.

name: William Brown Baxley
motto: "Much ado there was, God wot! He would love and she would not."
clubs: First Lieutenant Company B, R. O. T. C. S.
description: Bax is our idea of a public-spirited citizen. He has been always ready to do his share for the best interests of the class and University, serving with equal willingness as leader or follower. And those of us who remember Lieut. Baxley's efforts to enlist student support for military training and have watched his antics as cheer leader must admit that Bax has developed a capacity for leadership. The only time Brown shows a disposition to shirk his responsibility is when Dr. Barnett calls upon Baxley and doesn't specify whether he means W. B. or C. H. Then W. B. becomes quite retiring and is willing to let brother Herbert do it all. On the other hand when he and his bosom friend "The Swede" indulge in a verbal argument, the only noise comparable to it for intensity is Thompson's sneeze. We may add also that much of Chesley's nervousness is directly chargeable to Baxley.

name: Black, Charles Warren
motto: "His wit invites you by his looks to come, But when you knock, it never is at home." —Cowper.
description: Among his associates, Warren is known as "The Boy Wonder" of the C. E. department. One would not notice his extreme youth from a casual glance, but from a quiet and serious young man he has become a rival of Stanley Howard as the prize wit of the engineering school. Warren's chief student activities are drawing and cutting classes. Give him pencil and paper and he can reproduce anything he has ever seen and many things no one has seen, such as the "Australian Dik-Dik." The excellence of his technique led to his election as Art Editor of the Hullabaloo and the drawings in this book show that he is no mean artist. In his own estimation, Warren is the laziest of the C. E. Seniors and hence of the Engineers. But his problems are all drawn up in many-colored inks and his perfect notes are the envy of his classmates. His chief claim to distinction is the fact that he can make more strange noises than anyone except Howard, his best imitation being the long drawn "A-a-w-k" of a hen laying an egg. His cheerfulness of disposition is also unfailing. But speaking seriously, Warren is really a very talented young man and ought to make his mark in his profession. In the years to come we shall look back with pleasure on our acquaintance with Warren Black, C. E.

name: Brooks, James Vincent
motto: "He hasn't uttered a word as yet, But the air's astir with his praises."
description: To think that we have to tell you about Doc Brooks. Haven't you ever seen him play football? Ah, quite right—none other than the famous Captain Brooks himself looks out so sternly from the black spot in the upper corner. None other than "Cold Weather" Brooks, whose reputation alone brings countless scented notes to Hopkins, and who is sought by the ladies from Forest Park to Lexington, Kentucky, a full two cent stamp away. But Doc does other things at Hopkins besides play football and lacrosse. He is an O. Δ. K. man and really something of a student. And besides all of this J. V. is typical of the kind of fellows of whom '17 is proud. A clean sportsman, a courteous gentleman, and a man.

name: Bryan, Guy Lee
description: Guy is a quiet but pleasant chap with a natural ability to think and work with rapidity. And certainly Fortune has smiled upon him in thus endowing him, for he has found it necessary to spend the greater part of the last four years at Cambridge; on one of his recent visits there he is said to have carried a handsome diamond ring. Consequently Guy has had so little time to devote to such secondary matters as college courses that, possessed of less ability, he could never have graduated in four years. But we have found him always a thoroughly likeable and unselfish individual. His good disposition was nearly upset when from his surname Stanley Howard evolved successively O'Brien, Mike O'Brien, and Mike Muldoon. For this exhibition of wit Guy and "Fritz" Wood made Stanley buy the sodas for two weeks. Guy's intention is to become a civil engineer and accompanying our best wishes goes a prediction of his success.

name: William Downes Cecil
motto: "When you cheered your messmates nine with 'Ben Bolt' and Clementine." And "Dixie Land" and "Seeing Nellie Home."
clubs: Chairman of M.E. Branch of Engineering Society S.
Second Lieutenant Company B, R.O.T.C., S.
description: Even though he came to us with a Western Maryland A.B., something (the A.B. and not the W.M.) to be looked upon with awe and reverence by timid freshmen, for months no one except some old acquaintances even realized that Bill,--Preacher Bill Cecil--even existed. So unassuming, so quiet and reserved was he that it was not, properly speaking, until his junior year that it dawned upon us that we had a musician, a fusser, a wit and an all-around good fellow in our midst. Bill isn't built for athletics, he would make good on any team if he were--but he is built for creating a happy atmosphere, and no one can appreciate the smile of this genial individual unless he had heard him sing some of his darky melodies to the accompaniment of his own guitar. Those of us who have been lucky enough to get closer to him have found in him an industrious student, a genial messmate, and a true friend, and we hope and are confident that when the last "taps" sounds for Lieutenant William Cecil he will have left behind him a mark.

name: Ho, Chin Chen
motto: "No tickee, no washee!" — Goldberg.
clubs: Chairman Foreign Student Department of Y. M. C. A., S.
description: Ho Chin Chen is not a laundry sign but the name of one of the most likable members of our class. Although he hails directly from the yellow orient his faculty for grasping the English language was such that he not only took all of the lecture courses, but also took notes, and in our native script at that. For proof but look at his record in Philosophy 1, where often our learned professor blared forth in bursts of oratory requiring a dictionary to interpret. Chen is of small build and was admitted into the "Five Foot Club" by a unanimous vote. Although he has not taken part in many university activities we feel that he has been a help to us in other ways. His record as a good student and his bright cheery smile with his quaint little phrases we all shall remember.

name: Charles Webster Chesley
motto: "My hopes no more must change their name, I long for a repose that ever is the same."
clubs: Captain Company B, R. O. T. C., S.
description: Ever since this little sand burr from St. Mary's first hove into sight he's been telling us of how it's done down there. It took his bosom friend, Tommy, just three hours after meeting him to confer upon him the only name by which he is known to many of us,--"Down Home." To others he is intimately referred to as "Chigger." "Lawd my eyes hurt me--I gotta lay down and restum," and in five minutes he is snoring serenely. This is his greatest outstanding characteristic--his desire for sleep, and if, by the irony of fate, he must needs stand a long night shift on an engine test, it takes him about three nights at twelve hours slumber per, to resatiate his soul with what he is pleased to call bliss,--not of the W. J. A. variety, however. With grave doubts, we wonder if "Down Home," will ever be what he says he is going to be--an engineer; for his talents are so diversified,--orator, poet, farmer, CHEMIST, &c, &c. But there is one thing that we are sure that he will always be,--a good pal to tie to in all sorts of weather. Everyone likes him, and he is one of the few members of the class who have won any degree of popularity without working themselves to death.

name: Hyman Abraham Cohen
motto: "Patience, and shuffle the cards." —Cervantes.
description: It has been said that the man who keeps his mouth shut at the right time is a good talker. If this were the only criterion, Cohen would be an orator, for very few of the members of the class have ever heard him speak. But it will be remembered of freshman year that on certain occasions in Physics I Cohen grew very expressive. Those few who know, claim that when once started in a small gathering he is a veritable phonograph. However we must demand proof of this claim before we can believe it. Cohen is so quiet that it wasn't until his third year that we knew he was in the class. We have heard that Hyman is quite a mathematician which would seem reasonable since we always look for a greater display of brains by the quiet sort. If he should ever decide to become more communicative, we do not doubt that he could give us much helpful information.

name: Bernard Herman Conn
motto: "A true philosopher is beyond the reach of fortune." —Laudor.
clubs: Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
description: Conn was overlooked in the organization of the Five-Foot Club. We are reminded of this fact by Dr. Vincent's method of quizzing his class in medieval history. Anyone who has had a course of history under Dr. Vincent is aware of how he at times glances around the room to endeavor to catch the eye of someone to answer his question. Conn is usually either not seen or — well, we had better say that he is slouched down behind someone more gigantic than he. At any rate, it is a very rare occurrence that the class gets the opportunity to hear from him. If you were to ask Conn what he would especially emphasize in defense of college life, he would unhesitatingly answer, "its tendency to broaden one's outlook on life." This statement is characteristic of him. He looks for broadening influences in all matters. Those who are more intimately acquainted with Conn know that he is an earnest and industrious person. We believe that after college, he intends to take his place in the business world, and we feel certain that wherever his activities will call him, he will honorably and creditably represent his University.

name: Crist, Francis Murray
description: A bold, bad man! When the students from Poly entered the University in our Sophomore year, there was among them one rebellious spirit which we at once undertook to subdue. Threats were of no avail, and even dumping him into Jake's towel-basket did not completely conquer him. So after a period of probation he, together with those who had come here with him, was admitted to our class organization. Crist has a greater capacity for enjoyment than any of the rest of us judging from the amount of time he takes off for that purpose. Whenever he has no class and sometimes oftener, he goes downtown for a session at the Maryland, where he has a better attendance record than even Junkins. This is perhaps a good thing, for his fellow C.E.'s are afraid that he will exert an evil influence on Smitty. Of outdoor sports he prefers baseball and likes to catch behind the bat. So far as we know, his daily life begins and ends at Union Station. We do gather from outside sources that he lives in the commonwealth of Sparrows Point, where he observes sufficient to tell the professors a thing or two about their subjects, and is a leader in society. Whatever our first impression of him was, we think that he has improved greatly during his stay here, and hope that he will be the president of a steel plant some day.

name: Thomas Morris Cushing
motto: "Look, then, into thine heart and write!" —Longfellow.
description: Cushing is the only representative from the famous Maryland town of Woodbrook who has deigned to honor our class, and from our association with him we feel that if the remainder of the community are like him the loss of not having others is all ours. Cushing is a peculiar person in that he combines within his makeup so many varied characteristics: notably the ability to write good English, to indulge in witty sarcasms, and to raise chickens. This is quite an array of valuable natural endowments, and he is not sparing the use of them. We may say also that Cushing started his career at this university as a student in Group 1,—that is, as a student with a fondness for Latin and Greek, but, by his fourth year, he had entirely deserted the classics. Cushing is a rather quiet and retiring young man and for this reason he will always be given less credit than he deserves. In this, however, he is rather fortunate for the man who receives more than his share of laurels, always meets, some time or other, the conviction of his unreservedness, while the man of the other type always has the opportunity to pleasantly surprise his associates. We confidently await one of these pleasant surprises in the future from Cushing.

name: J. Wilmerton Darley
clubs: Captain Company A R. O. T. C., S.
description: The women simply adored him; his lips were like Cupid's bow; But he never ventured to use them and so they voted him slow. Hello, Darley, just getting in? this is the daily salutation with which the class greets J. Wilmerton, for be it known that this young man holds the distinction of being late to every nine o'clock class he ever attended with the exception of two,—one when he sat up all night on a boiler test, and once on the day that the uniforms were expected. No matter how much we bawl him out, as per above quotation, he merely grins and does it again, and, we hand it to him, he surely has the faculty cowed. This is his chief characteristic, but he has others as for example his ability to call football signals, and when it came to using his head, as one paper put it, he is a general with a big G. When the management doled out numbers to each player Will drew No. 7, and as a result has been called everything from Old Mysterious to Natural. Other characteristics: the best form displayed on the track without getting anywhere with it; his coal black, stand-on-end hair; his Beau Brummel appearance in his uniform; and his weakness for spending nickels on the Park Heights Avenue Car Line. For none of these do we blame him, and we feel sure that if you could meet her, you would be willing to spend a nickel too.

name: Allan Conradt Davis
motto: "A life of ease is a difficult pursuit." —Cowper.
clubs: Member of 5th Regiment Games Committee S.
Secretary S. A. I. A. A., S.
description: Allan by his first three years of work at Hopkins won the reputation of being far-sighted and has indeed lived up to his rating. It is probably due to this characteristic that those connected with Allan in the various activities in which he participates feel safe in autoing with him, and as a result he is quite frequently given a chance to make use of his several motors. If it is not an automobile it is that device best described as a cross between a full-grown bicycle and a young motor-truck. Allan was chosen to manage the 1917 football team no doubt due to his exceptional qualities in the above-named line, and ran true to form in predicting that the team would again have the services of Jack Branham—and it did! The move that Davis made by which he won credit as a reader of the future was in changing from the engineering course to the easy-going life of an A.B. while still a Soph. His fellow classmates who were unable to change over (not for a minute that they ever deserved to) still remind him of the real pleasure that he missed by not following his first intentions. It may be with a feeling of envy, brought on by being forced to attend a laboratory each afternoon when Davis leisurely takes a pleasure ride, that is behind this statement, but regardless of the incentive it is direct from the shoulder. One of Allan's other delights is to participate in the musical club's concerts and, as one of the mainstays of the club, really mystifies the close observer by his clever method of wielding the pick over his mandola strings. Allan is a capable fellow and is a member of the "jest nat'chlly bright" class. He has always taken more than his share of interest in college matters and has won the close friendship of all of his associates.

name: Dawson, Frank William
motto: "I am satisfied to trifle away my time rather than let it stick to me." —Pope.
description: Undoubtedly the most carefree member of the class of 1917 is F. W. Dawson. Those of us who have attended with him know him as a conceiver of new ideas. Sometimes these new ideas concern his course of study and sometimes they do not. However, we believe that for the most part he adopts the latter of the two general kinds. For example, let us cite the incident which occurred on the geology trip of 1915, when he nearly convinced the inhabitants of Cumberland that the party was made up of University professors, and that their sole object was the finding of gold. Other similar pranks are easily traced to this natural source, the dissecting of cats and craw-fish being only a secondary occupation of his. Dawson is, however, settling down and we believe that before those long locks turn gray he will have become famous in other lines of endeavor. Be that as it may, we are glad that the change did not come too soon, as we have enjoyed his fun and frolics immensely.

name: Defandorf, John L.
motto:
"His bark is worse than his bite."
—*Herbert.*
clubs: H baseball F., So., J., S.; Captain of Varsity Baseball Team S.; Member of Class Baseball Team F., So.; Captain of Class Baseball Team So.; Class Treasurer J.; Member of 1917 Hullabaloo Board S.; Delegate to Athletic Board S.; Member of Athletic Investigation Committee S.; Member of Engineering Society J., S.; Student Member of A. S. M. E.
description: He first stepped into the limelight when, due to the illness of Sylvester and Pfeiffer, Coach Eddie Hooper sent him to the mound, a wee, untried freshman, to pitch against St. Johns. If Jno. L. had any misgivings he didn't show them and after allowing the Crabtowners five scattered hits in nine innings, he walked off the field with their scalp stowed safely away. Sophomore year he repeated the feat; Junior year illness prevented his playing; Senior year the doughty little captain will no doubt get another chance and will come through again. But enough of "minor sports," let's proceed to his major. Romping with Eddie Stuart to the vast delight of the fellow-inhabitants of the M. E. 3 draughting-room is his greatest recreation, but those who know him more intimately declare there is a greater,—worrying himself "Whiteheaded" over transformer designs. His greatest honor is his Captaincy of the 1917 baseball team; his greatest attribute his attraction for women; his greatest fault that he hails from the outskirts of Washington,—Baltimore's most beautiful suburb.

name: John Emil Andrew Doermann
motto:
O let me live my own, and die so too,
To live and die is all I have to do.
description: When John packed his knapsack and wended his way to Hopkins from Washington, he was the most innocent, immaculate-souled, guileless, and upright freshman in the class—excepting only Louie Naylor. But oh, the tragedy of life! First he joined the chess club and knocked out one of the props from the foundation of his blamelessness. Then he bought himself a pipe with real tobacco and knocked out another prop. Having degenerated to such low depths as to watch the boys pitch nickels without the slightest tremor of conscience, he went the limit and bought himself some Red ties, which he wore every day to his dining palace at Klitch's. John is now a graduate and will return home with his diploma in his knapsack. O Mores! O Tempora! Having systematized and methodized his modus operandi, how can Washington make for itself a coat of mail of sufficient strength to ward off his machinations? Yet the University has done John much good. He is clean cut through and through (as far as students go), and it has been a real pleasure to associate with him. We like him for his sincerity and for his frankness and are glad to say that he belongs to 1917. But how we do weep for those fallen props!

name: William Bantz Dürr
motto: "For a man seldom thinks with more earnestness of anything than he does his dinner." —Sam Johnson
description: We used to call this chap "Germany," but since diplomatic relations have been broken off and we are so close to the brink of the great abyss, we must look for another prefix for this Dutchman. He has been a great help to us throughout our stay here, especially to Straus, in front of whom he sits in Pol. Econ. I., thus allowing our worthy editor many a pleasant nap. He has also helped us out in basketball, where he took a stellar position, helping the seniors to win the championship for the second consecutive year. In the summer he is seen rambling aimlessly around the vicinity of Baltimore, with a young trunk under each arm, trying to persuade young housewives to invest in aluminum; while in the winter he is always to be found in "Walter's Art Gallery" or playing chess. We have seen him at odd times drink milk shakes at Doc Adams' fountain, but we believe that another form of liquid food is really responsible for all that good old German heft.

name: Edlavitch, Ernest
motto: "What a spendthrift he is of his tongue." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Menorah Society F., So., J., S.
Championship Class Baseball Team J., S.
description: Almost every student in the 1917 class has felt the pull of some subject during his college career. Eddie is one of those who have been attracted into biology and in 1921 expects to receive his M.D. His only form of physical activity is playing basketball which he would far rather do than study. He has been one of the mainstays on our class team ever since the league was organized and helped win the championship for us in our junior year. Upon all occasions when work at the University is suspended for a time, Ernest retires to his summer capital at Bowie. He can frequently be seen driving the family car up and down the country roads in the vicinity of that beautiful and progressive Southern Maryland town. This youngster was anything but a promising student when he entered our Alma Mater. In Biology I his eternal question was; "Is it a quarter after yet?" (It takes Dr. Andrews fifteen minutes to say anything worth listening to, and Eddie was accustomed to sleep that long at the beginning of each lecture). He has, however, found himself and is no longer a sleepy countryman, but a live-wire student. And if a word of prophecy may be indulged in, we may look for this "Kid" to become a prominent physician in the middle west — some twenty years from now.

name: Eugene Albert Edgett
motto: "Bid me discourse, I will enchant thine ear, Or, like a fairy, trip upon the green." —Venus and Adonis.
description: His legs are so long he looks like a pair of scissors, but this is not a defect. On the contrary it should be posted in the asset column, for these shanks are directly responsible for his ability as a half miler, which, by the way, is no mean one. Edgett has gone through Hopkins in three years, entering a year after most of the men in our class, but he did it well and with marks ranging from 9.5 to 10 plus. Also he is a debater of no mean ability, but he shines brightest on the dance floor, where we must admit he can certainly make those aforementioned legs behave. We think Eugene will be a professor, because of the ease with which he separates the School Board from the five-dollar bills when a substitute is needed at the City College, but you can't always tell. Maybe he'll remain human after all. Whichever way he chooses, however, we wish him all kinds of luck and are glad he came out with "Seventeen."

name: Raymond Wilson Evitt
motto: "O Music, sphere-descended maid, Friend of Pleasure, Wisdom's aid." —Collins.
description: Almost everybody (in fact this young man himself) believes that Wilson is destined to become one of the world's foremost mechanical engineers some day. Everything he says—everything he does would lead one to that conclusion. But it is all a delusion! Ev. has one great masterful passion which will eventually assert itself. Of this we are confident although owing to his extreme modesty, he has hidden his genius from the outside world. But those few who have been so extremely fortunate as to have heard this dark-eyed, dreamy soul touch a piano keyboard will agree with the writer that it would be a tragedy for the fingers which produced the exquisite melody, and have the delicate touch of a budding artist to be associated with anything so unrefined and coarse as a buzz-saw or a riveting machine. Take care, Wilson old top, that you don't lose one of those valuable fingers. Better follow the straight and narrow way and develop that dormant musical talent. The engineering field was never intended for the artist.

name: Warfield Monroe Firor
motto: "High diddle-diddle, the Cat and the Fiddle, The Cow jumped over the Moon." —Mother Goose.
clubs: Vice-president Y. M. C. A., J.
description: Firor is a man of versatile nature, who exhibits a fondness for both athletics and study. For two years Monnie has been a valuable member of the track team, and while engaged in practicing during his senior year developed an almost unlimited capacity for smashing hurdles as well as high jump records. All through his college life he has been a devotee of science and by this time can analyze a complex chemical substance, state involved laws of physics, or dissect a cat better, probably, than any member of his class. As can be readily conjectured from a glimpse of him, Firor is very good-natured, and has a sense of humor. His face is always ready to expand into a grin, especially in room 304 (Ma's). We understand that Monnie is going to the Medical School, and we feel sure that his broad smile and good nature will help greatly to cheer the sick whom he may call upon. He is bound to make a place for himself, which will reflect much credit on the class of 1917.

name: Robert France
motto: I weigh an ounce or two, and I'm so small You can pack me in the pocket of your vest.
clubs: Official Johns Hopkins 5th Regiment Track Meet F.
Second Lieutenant Company C, R. O. T. C., S.
Chairman of Ushers at Interclass Debate S.
description: Bobby, a charter member by common consent, of the "Five Foot Club" is known and liked by all who know him for his cheerfulness. It fairly radiates from him and, as one of his professors once remarked, he is always—burgues—a good-liver. His chief worry seems to be getting the inter-fraternity board to hold meetings, and his chief joys seem to be kidding along his profs. and flying around in his "Scripps." Although he has never been known to overburden himself with any great amount of work, except before an exam., he always comes up smiling, and we feel sure that if he ever gets thoroughly interested in anything he will have plenty of push to put behind it.

name: Frank, Harry F. W.
motto: "Constant thought will overflow in words, unconscientiously." -Byron.
clubs: Class Numerals in Football and Lacrosse So.
description: Harry is one of the few members of our class who thought that his education would be incomplete without Greek. That is why in his Senior year he gave so much time to this subject and how he developed the ability to take wild chances with the "dead languages." Since Frank was compelled to give up football, he has taken up "basement oratory" and has been often seen, pipe at hand, as speaker or audience, together with the other members of the Philosophy 2., at these verbal "Battles of the post-office." Apart from the hours wasted in this manner, Harry has been industrious and has given evidence of considerable ability in other lines. He is rather erratic, however, and forges ahead in spurts. In his senior year he had more of these sudden movements of spirit than ever before, and there is considerable hope that he may be entering into a period of permanent inspiration. Harry is both congenial and popular, and we wish the best of success in the profession he is to enter.

name: Thomas McCauley Gontrum
motto: "Of all kinds of ambition, that which pursues poetical fame is the wildest." --Goldsmith.
description: One may chance to meet Gontrum in the basement almost any time—especially when not expecting him. His chief diversion at the University is reading the notices near the post-office; his chief occupation is "library browsing" and at this occupation he toils by the hour. His fund of general information is very large as a result of the time spent in the reading of heterogeneous subjects. He can carry on a very interesting conversation on practically any topic, and moreover can back up his statements by copious references,—but the hard part of the whole matter is to get him into a conversational humor. Aside from his University work, Gontrum coquettes to some extent with the Muses. To be more exact, we mean that he finds his chief amusement in the writing of poetry. Of course we are very glad of the fact for a poet always proves useful, and there is a large degree of probability that we may need Gontrum in the future to immortalize our names, and his own, with the Roman and Greek dignitaries. We hope that his name will live as long.

name: Howard Jennings Gorman
motto: "How, Sir! not damn the sharper, but the dice?" —Pope.
clubs: Varsity Football Team, F., So., J., S.
Varsity Baseball Team, F., So., J., S.
Chairman Class Banquet Committee F., So., J.
Member Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
Captain Class Football Team, F., So.
Member Class Lacrosse Team, F., So.
Member Class Track Team, F., So.
description: Tall, stately, serious,—with a dreamy look in his eye, one would expect to see wings sprouting from "Eggy" to look at him—but to know him, to observe him,—what a difference. Let someone mention "African Golf" (at which game he is a past master) and straightway there is no holding him. Let the resonous "plump" of a well kicked football, or the resounding "smack" of a baseball on a bat come to his ears and Eggy's GONE. The lure of both games came to him early and he has been a mainstay on the gridiron and diamond. Indeed in athletics generally he has done himself proud, for who can forget the day he so cooly walked away with the mile for 1917, leaving the '16 aspirants so far in the rear that for the most part they gave up in disgust. But the greatest thing he ever did for us was to manage three of the best little banquets ever held by a Hopkins Class, the radiance of the first being greatly augmented by the lustre of his multicolored eye (the result of a friendly scuffle with certain Sophs), and that of the last by the brilliance of his wit as toastmaster.

name: Hall, Elbridge Gerry
motto: "It would talk; Lord, how it talked!" —Beaumont and Fletcher.
clubs: Class numerals baseball, track, and lacrosse
Member of the track team 1915, 1916 and 1917
description: Never met Gerry, you say? Well you're out of luck! Let me introduce you to the most care-free, happy-go-lucky, spontaneously happy man in the class, Mr. Gerry Hall. For three long years we knew him only as "catching up"; that was his chronic state, but now he has chosen to elect the most difficult of Engineering subjects, Electricity, we are surprised to hear that it is now a case of "Gerry-on-the-job." "Seen Gerry Hall's new hair cut?" This, once a month, is the pregnant question at Homewood, for should one miss seeing the latest thing in this line he simply must feel that his education is being neglected. He tries a different kind every time, and while they all have the same characteristic of allowing each individual hair to slope backward at an angle of 45 degrees, he always manages to have it cut a bit differently around the edges, usually scalloped. Also, Hall is quite a runner, surprising his friends by winning the 220 open in the Armory Meet a couple of years ago. Following this feat he became an ardent trackster, and continued winning races (at intervals when the Black-list did not interfere) throughout the rest of his Hopkins career. He is an ardent supporter of all sports and we prophesy and hope that he will be wearing an "H" by the time this book goes to press.

name: Frederick Perrott Hall
motto: "There goes the man." —Persius.
clubs: Associate Editor of News-Letter F., So., J., S.
President of Y. M. C. A., J.
Chairman of Committee on Prep.-School Visiting S.
description: This tall, athletic, crimson-headed individual is one of the very few representatives in our class from Tome School, and if they have any more like him up there we should be very glad to correspond with them, for "Freddy" has always been one of the best men in the class both from the standpoint of scholarship and athletics. He is generally pointed out as the man who holds the Hopkins half mile record and he carries his ability as a runner into all the walks of life, and among those who are more intimate with him he is known as "Rush" Hall. Every week end there is a marked lack of Fred at the University and at his Fraternity House, and upon inquiring into the cause of this singular absence, we find that he makes mysterious trips to Washington at that time, to see his family,—so he says, and we might add that in our belief that is about all he does do, SEE his family, for we have it straight from one who knows that the cause of these trips is the same that makes Darley (see page 41) throw his nickels away so ruthlessly to the conductors on the Park Heights Avenue Line. Whatever may be said of Fred Hall, he has been a man worthy of 1917, and as Captain of the University Track Team and President of this class we have never found him lacking in any way.

name: Hobelmann, Herman August
motto: "He who is good is happy." —Habbington.
description: Holbe is somewhat older than the rest of the members of his class, and the dignity of age has probably held him aloof from some of the ordinary activities in which many of us have been engaged. However, if a trifle dignified, he still likes us and is a sturdy member of the school of basement philosophers and wits. To the chosen few among whom he unbends, his imitations of popular and peculiar profs are greatly admired and encouraged. Holbe has his own idea of courses, no labs for him, —and of profs. Of some of the latter he is not in favor and he is not in the least adverse to telling them so. This little oddity of character has led to some classic encounters between Hobelman and Professor. As a student Herr Herman is quite conscientious and it is to be utterly deplored, that in some language courses,—to which courses Holbe is passionately addicted,—a certain hesitancy of speech in the presence of a difficult passage for translation becomes malignant. Let it be said that the other members of the class and also the professor struggle in sympathy. Holbe is big, kind and jovial and although we do not know what he wants in the future, we hope that, whatever it is, he gets it.

name: Hopkins, Henry Hinkson
motto: "Let us never forget that the cultivation of the earth is the most important labor of man."
description: So quiet, unassuming, and unobtrusive has "Hop" been in his dealings with us, that when the time came for us to write for you the story of his college career, we were stumped at once. No one on the board, no one of his apparently most intimate friends could find a thing on him, and so we regret to say that we will have to pass him up to you as a perfectly model young gentleman, being unable to quote a single fault, vice, or habit of his; a thing which is unheard of, — in this book at least. We do know that he lives out in the wilds of Harford County and that he commutes and, although we do not dare to vouch for the truth of the statement, we are told by a fellow villager of his that during his Freshman Year he had a great deal of difficulty waking up on the outward trip in time to get off at his regular stopping place. The result was that on several occasions he had to walk several miles before being able to crawl into his "downey." Now, however, the conductors are so used to finding him asleep, that they kick him off without further ado for which favor "Hop" is truly thankful. Whatever may be the cause of this unusual drowsiness, and there seems but one plausible one, we forgive him and wish with all our hearts that there were more like him in the class.

name: Houghton, Ira Luther
motto: "O silken hair that veils the sunny brow! O eyes of gray, so tender and so true."
clubs: Member Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
President of the Combined Musical Clubs
description: Picture to yourself a large pair of tortoise-shell glasses, some waves of sandy hair, a rolling gait, and a puff of cigarette smoke, and you have a "movie" of L. Houghton, Esq. Of all the nonchalant, blase ducks you ever met, he is the beatenest. Living upon the theory that "if pleasure interferes with business, give up your business," he has been frequently heard to remark, just after mid-years, "well, I didn't exactly pull a ten, but I got by so why give-a-damn." He thinks he is going to be a Doctor, but we rather think not; there is something too "stiff" about that business; better try something more graceful and artistic.

name: Howard, Stanley Lewis
motto: "A little curly-headed, good for nothing, and mischief-making monkey from his birth. —Byron, Don Juan."
clubs: Chairman, C. E. Branch of Engineering Society, S.
description: There are very few people who, upon meeting this mannerly young man for the first time, can believe that he has any of the characteristics of a comedian. But those of us who have been most closely associated with him are unanimously of the opinion that Stanley can make more funny faces, twist himself into more peculiar positions, and emit more unseemly noises than any other person in the University. He can imitate all the machinery in the shop, in addition to several kinds of birds and animals. When he and "Fritz" Wood start their clamor at the same time, the rabble reminds one of feeding time at the zoo. Stanley and Fritz are partners in the tracing and blue-printing business. Both are neat draftsmen, and they probably would have been very successful if each hadn't spent so much time trying to argue the other into doing all the work. "Mac" has always been popular among his classmates and was chosen chairman of the C. E. branch of the Engineering Society. We are safe in predicting that his popularity will continue wherever he may be placed, and certainly the best wishes of us all go with him.

name: Chester Ezekiel Hurwitz
motto: "Fat, fair, and forty — Scott."
clubs: Member Menorah Society, So., J., S.
description: Hurwitz's real ambition in life is to become a doctor but we are led to believe that he would be more of a success as a salesman. Chester has developed an ability to sell more of his old books and receive a greater price for them than any other member of the class. With remarkable adroitness he will seize upon some unsophisticated freshman and use such plausible arguments that the poor freshie closes the deal at once and thinks he has a bargain. Hurwitz also tries to assume a don't-care attitude toward his lessons but he cannot fool his classmates on that. They all know he takes particular care to get his work done and is always afraid of not sliding through exams. However, his worries do not seem to affect his general health and after four years of college life he can still tip the scales at 200 pounds.

name: Albert B. Junkins
motto: "He shed a radiant smile around and smoked a rank cigar." —Service.
description: The mere mention of the name Junkins calls to mind black cigars, an excitable individual, and an industrious student. "Junk" has been smoking cigars ever since he entered the University and we don’t know how long before. The quality of the smokes the reader may judge for himself when we relate that Junkins presented one to Dr. Hulburt, who after scrutinizing it, remarked that he didn’t know whether the donation was an expression of affection or a grudge. However, these stogies seem to aid "Kate’s" powers of concentration because he is a most assiduous student and has always ranked among the highest in the class. He is very pugnacious under the stress of excitement and on one occasion threatened to whip the whole class for shaving a dear little mustache from his upper lip. But aside from this peculiarity, Junkins is an agreeable individual and has been a worthy member of 'Seventeen.

name: William Kendall, Jr.
motto: "I drink when I have occasion, and sometimes when I have no occasion. —Cervantes."
description: Bill Kendall reminds one, at first sight, of a portrayal of the typical detective, slinking along with shoulders slightly stooped and pipe clenched firmly between his teeth. But in the four years that we have been together we found Bill to be not of such an enquiring nature as his appearance suggests, but a congenial at times humorous classmate. Bill has never been called a "grind" by any of us, and what thoughts go on behind that semi-amused expression of his can only be guessed. His attitude in "Slony's" classes is most enigmatic. Slony refers to him as a heathen—possibly because Bill never takes notes and appears to be chuckling inwardly just at the time when Socrates is becoming most dialectic. Maybe these smile-producing thoughts are of the lecture being given and maybe they are of some of the originals of the pictures he carries around with him—who knows! Perhaps they are of the former, but we think the latter—anyhow we've got to give him credit for getting through on a minimum and keeping off the "black-list."

name: Knipp, John Christian
motto: "He's of stature somewhat low — Your hero always should be tall, you know. — Churchill."
clubs: Class Numerals, Football, Baseball, Track, Lacrosse
Charter Member Omicron Delta Kappa Circle J.
President of Omicron Delta Kappa Circle, S.
Member Y. M. C. A. Deputation Team, S.
Secretary J. H. U. Athletic Investigation Committee, S.
description: Small, dapper and as full of pep as Italian stew is of garlic, John tries everything and makes a success of it. The only thing he hasn't done is keep still, and whether he's playing lacrosse or managing the News-Letter, or taking minutes for Dr. Goodnow's Athletic Investigating Committee, he's Johnny-on-the-Jump every second. Among other things, and that probably the greatest honor of them all, John Christian is President of the Omicron Delta Kappa Society at Hopkins. How in the world such a little fellow came in for such a lion's share of laurels can be easily explained by the personal equation— Pep plus ability plus downright likeableness equals popularity times success. J. C. is an example of how parties and dances help a man make a success of life, and proves that a steady attendant at such functions is the surest way to land in the midst of our people. His one-time professors would probably not admit this, but nevertheless it's true. If it wasn't there would be no Knipp tucked in this book and Johnny would have been an alumnus sometime previous to the time of our leave-taking. We don't have to wish him success; he'll grab it. We won't wish him happiness, for he radiates it. So we bid him an adieu with the comfortable feeling that we know he'll do it, whatever it is, and will do it in a way '17 will never regret.

name: Levin, Jacob
motto: "Whose little body lodged a mighty mind. —Homer."
major: School of Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, Gas Engineering
description: Jake is a clever boy and so small, too, but with huge and massive ideas. It does seem paradoxical that such big thoughts should come from the small head which serves the purpose of preventing Jake's spinal cord from unraveling. But he gets away with it and deserves the credit. Mechanical Engineering attracted him for a time, but he soon mastered this branch and looked for a less developed field in which his originality might receive some recognition. Thus during his last year at Hopkins he spent a great amount of time for a fellow of such small stature in the study of Gas Engineering. Regardless of what influenced him to take up the study of gas, etc., we are in a position to say that he is not wasting his time for before he had gone very far into the subject he could say more of nothing in a set time than the average person. Seriously Jake did accomplish worthy results in a branch of engineering that was not thoroughly developed at Hopkins, and it was through his efforts that the gas plant received immediate attention. Here's fortune to one who was so popular among his classmates.

name: Morris Levin
motto: "Oh sleep! it is a gentle thing, Beloved from pole to pole!" —Coleridge.
description: Why has he no middle name? Well, that is quite easy. I dare say that even Strobel could answer that question correctly. It is this—Levin is an ardent believer in the theory of the conservation of energy, and in looking into the future saw the useless effort that would be expended in signing the intermediate initial (he certainly would not have used the entire name no matter what it was), and so just naturally left it out. His every move bears out his belief in this theory, and through the aid of the conservation hypothesis the question of why he, together with Darley, is invariably late for a nine o'clock class is solved. His instructors in said classes have accepted his hypothesis, and feeling assured that he will be on hand a few minutes before the hour ends, mark him late. Another trait which was for a time characteristic of Morris was to smoke cheap cigars in the drafting room. Of course, it was impossible for other serious-minded engineers to concentrate upon their work, and some wise one devised a plan for ending this habit. It was to accept his offer of the vile weeds. Soon the offers stopped, only to be followed by requests for another smoke by the few who could hold on to the cigar after a few puffs. The end of Levin's smoking in the presence of his classmates was not far off, but think of the hardship that the noble heroes who took part in the smoking of his special brand were forced to undergo.

name: Walter Cecil Loague
description: Often in the calm and peaceful walks of life, far off from every suggestion from the noise and smoke of battle, we find men who are facing successfully objects much greater than any which Mars with all his fiendish cleverness can produce. In this category of men who have successfully met the more deeply rooted difficulties of peace, we feel that Loague is well entitled to a position. Fired on by a divine spark of ambition, he has toiled to the limit of human endurance. We know that there have been periods, each extending over several weeks, in which he kept up his studies by day and the office work of a local corporation by night, averaging about three hours sleep out of every twenty-four. But he has determinedly and steadily mounted even though handicapped by lack of the "sleep that knits up the raveled sleeve of care." For his life work, Loague intends to enter the ministry and we know that in this field his sincerity and earnestness will be appreciated. Toil, however, must have its reward and we sincerely hope that in the future Loague's case may be like that of a preacher who Goldsmith said was: "Like some tall cliff that lifts its awful form, Swells from the vale, and midway leads the storm; Though round its breast the rolling clouds are spread, Eternal sunset settled on its head."

name: William Archibald Mckewen
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, J.
description: How Bill laughed and breathed a sigh of relief when the board of collegiate studies removed Physics I from the list of compulsory courses. He put off the evil day as long as possible and was freed from the only dread which college held for him when the above mentioned action was taken. We say that this was his only dread for Mac had the happy faculty of grasping literary, historical and language courses quite readily and without a great expenditure of intellectual energy. To Bill goes the credit for the success of our class basketball teams. He was chiefly interested in this branch of sport and did much to make the game popular at Hopkins. But Mac's real mastery is manifested in matters of love. When he is not in the University or at Calvert School there is one place where you will be certain to find him—at Goucher College. For it has been whispered about the campus that his ideal is a student there. It is very easy to discern now why Mac has suddenly become so sedate and serious, and why he refrains from partaking of any of our juvenile activities. Bill has been at all times a worker for the best interests of the University and the class of 1917 and will be remembered by his many friends as a thorough, clear-thinking fellow who is bound to make good.

name: Merrick, Robert Graff
motto: "The worse the man, the better the soldier." —Napoleon I.
clubs: Captain of Class Rush Team So.
Delegate to Cotillion Club F. So.
description: One of Bob's greatest accomplishments and one of the many things that he has done at Hopkins for which he deserves credit was to pass Ma Greene's course in English Lit. I. Spurred on by this victory Bob boldly tackled English Lit. 2 and really got away with it. The 9.5 which Mother gave him was not secured by the means which his middle name suggests, for he is not that sort of a fellow; at the same time we do not think that he studied to the extent of the above rating because—well for the same reason. He has the valuable asset of concentration and is not forced to rub noses with books as are some of his less fortunate classmates. Yes, on close inspection it is seen that Bob has the ear marks of an athlete. He was cut out for pigskin chaser, ball tosser, cinder-path artist, or some other form of devil on the athletic field, but evidently Bob has been unable to develop his ability due to the call of other activities. He was, throughout his undergraduate career, one of the most prominent and capable of his class. As a member of the Omicron Delta Kappa Society, president of the student council and of the cotillion board, to say nothing of the other honors bestowed upon him, he has been quite conspicuous. His prominence, however, is not of the tiring kind and we regret that our association with him must continue only as a memory.

name: Meyerhoff, Louis
motto: "Three-fifths of him genius and two-fifths sheer fudge." —Lowell.
clubs: Member Engineering Society So., J., S.
description: He is one of the few senior engineers who finds time for extra class work. He engages in no student activities and says that he needs something more to utilize all his energy. We wish that he had chosen some subject other than geology, for when he asks how your peneplain or dicellocephalis is, you imagine that you have a new sort of ache or pain. As previously intimated, Meyerhoff is not over-partial to work, especially of the manual variety. He would rather take notes than put on overalls and do the dirty part of the experiment. This suits his partners, who usually let him have the notebook. Louis commits one criminal act; he tries to sing, perhaps only in self-defense for there are several such offenders in the E. E. seniors' room. Meyerhoff sticks close to his books and has won an honorable mention twice, of which he is justly proud.

name: Mowbray, John Mcconkey
motto: "If I were a queen I would order Madame de Stael to talk to me all day." —Mme. de Tesse.
description: Jack is probably the most enigmatical chap in the class. All his friends unite in saying that he is the best of good fellows and that he is an enthusiastic Y. M. C. A. man. There seems to be no difference of opinion regarding the first part of the foregoing statement, but the significant tone in which Dr. Slonimsky remarked: "And they tell me you're a minister's son!" allows some room for doubt regarding the latter part. Thus the matter must stand unsolved, for Jack lives in Pikesville, just far enough removed from human habitation to preclude any attempts at investigation. Pikesville is probably the explanation of why Mowbray is likely to arrive in class any time between 9 A. M. and noon. We have heard from some sources that he will become a lawyer, while from others comes the report that he will do graduate work in experimental psychology. This question, however, like the other and for a similar reason remains, as yet, unanswered.

name: Naylor, Louis Hastings
description: Naylor, better known to his fellow-students as Louie, is perhaps one of the youngest, both in years and ways, of the members of our class. For some reason or other, it strikes us that Louie put on long trousers just before coming to Hopkins, and that he has been trying his best to grow into them during his stay here. By this time, they fit him pretty well; but even so, it seems that there is still a little space to be filled out. What Naylor lacks in body and years, however, he easily makes up in brains. He has a very clear understanding of linguistic matters, and is particularly strong in French and Latin. He is getting through the period of preparation well in the spring of life, and for this reason will have more time to devote to winning honor and glory for his university. We are sorry that we cannot tell you more of Louie in his own truly original and elevated tone, but unfortunately our own proclivities do not reach so high a scale.

name: Oppenheimer, Reuben
motto:
"Persuasion tips his tongue whene'er he talks."
—Colley Cibber.
clubs: Member Omicron Delta Kappa Circle, So., S.
description: Hats off to Rube! Here is another member of '17 who has done himself and the class proud and we're glad he is one of us. His excellent work as editor-in-chief of the News-Letter, together with his debating proclivities and general activity around the University, has won him no less an honor than the OΔK key; and he certainly deserves it. He and Johnny Knipp have given us the best paper in years, and his active brain played an important role in the winning of the inter-class debate from the Juniors. This same ability has also won him a place on the Varsity debating team. Another thing for which we shall remember the Rube is his active work in connection with the class endowment fund. Here again his modest air and angular figure served merely as a cloak for the silver lining, and Rube came across with the goods. He showed further evidence of excellent judgment by finishing at Hopkins in three years, hence graduating with "The Class". We admire his taste, and his presence adds another man of the successful type to our already brilliant roll.

name: Owings, Noble L.
motto: "He felt the ache of a lonely man to find a fitting mate."
clubs: Member Musical Clubs, F., So., S.
description: If you want anything done that needs doing, call on Owings. That semi-bald pate covers an active and fertile brain and a willing one, and in a pinch Noble always comes through with the goods. When you want a write-up in a hurry he does it; when half the musical club is late for a concert he takes its place, and entertains in such a grand or upright manner (as the case may be) that the audience is usually sorry when the other fellows turn up. Noble is the one restraining influence in the Senior C. E. department, and without him we fear that the chasing of the elusive "Wampae" and the following of the "Woos" hound would become a more frequent sport than the laying of sewers or the design of the Fink truss. As an associate editor of this book Noble has rendered invaluable service, and in fulfilling his other duties he has at all times been prompt and efficient. In short he is one of those men of whom we are justly proud and we hope we will all be present at the WEDDING.

name: Perkins, Edward Everett
motto: "Silence is golden"
clubs: Track Squad, F., So., J., S.
description: Someone has said that "Silence is golden" and if that be true, Doc is a pure gold nugget. When he opens his mouth it is for one of the three following purposes, given in the relative order of their frequency of occurrence: 1. To consume nourishment. 2. To gasp for breath at the close of a hard fought mile. 3. To speak. At rare intervals, and then only after Stoic forethought, he performs 3 and it is to be marked that having sufficiently deliberated, the laconic outcome is quite likely to be correct. This slow spoken type is generally determined in its manner and he is no exception to the rule. He stuck at track until he won that greatest of all athletic honors, "H" and has finally developed into one of Joe's most dependable milers. Poor old Perk; we understand that he has ambitions towards matrimony in the dim future, having confided that he considers this man's ultimate purpose, but how could a phlegmatic temperament like his ever summon sufficient emotion to make him talk enough to persuade her. We would suggest that he consult Jack Defandorf,—he's a "wuzzard" at that game!

name: Pitt, Charles Gordon
motto: "But thou art fair, and at thy birth, dear boy, Nature and Fortune join'd to make thee great." —King John.
clubs: Business Manager, Y. M. C. A. Handbook, J. S.
Assistant Business Manager, News-Letter, J., S.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, J., S.
Baseball H. A. A., So., J.
description: It was not very long after Gordon's arrival in the University back in the Fall of 1913 that he sprang into fame by the remarkable discovery of an extra joint in a bee's leg. This startling find was made in the Natural History Laboratory one afternoon, by virtue of which Gordon passed the course with a high mark,—not to mention being admitted as a "sniffer" by Alan Steinbach to the then famous Spirical Society. Sophomore year saw a complete change. From a serious pupil Gordon changed to one of those men who are in every student activity. He made Spieker reverse the Instrumental Club three times so that he could play the drums, and after tiring of music he entered the field of managers, managing everything in sight (but the musical clubs—much to Spieker's delight). He now gets more mail than anybody in the University. He has no bad habits—doesn't smoke, drink, or swear. Whether because of these virtues or not Gordon is always "right there," and if you ever want to go away and leave a thing to be done properly put it in his hands and you will not be disappointed when you return.

name: Milton Reiner
description: That Sphinx-like face, remote, austere, Let us all read, whatever the cost! When the time came to write a story about this youthful and budding Electrician, the only things which could be remembered of his college career were that he operated the lantern for the Engineering Society lectures and displayed rare form therein (witness lecture of Mr. Hinds on the Scavenging and Atomizing Diesel), that he is a persevering student; and that he is the only man in the class who won the distinction of being the boon companion of the aloof Wacker and the inscrutable Cohen. From these two gentlemen we have learned much concerning "Mickey's" prowess with the fair sex and it needs but one glance at his above likeness to convince one that there are grounds, at least, for believing this report. With the other students in his department we frequently hear him complaining of the amount of work that "Docteur Engineur" assigns them, but we can offer but little sympathy, for every time that we are called upon to visit their "sanctorium" the incense (?) of Reiner's pipe assails our nostrils, and if we are brave enough to enter we find him seated with Wacker and Cohen and any other who can be coerced into the party, playing that unceasing, untiring game of—well we'll call it 500 for diplomacy's sake.

name: Roop, John Daniel
motto: "His black coat off and his old clothes on, 'Now I'm myself,' says Farmer John."
description: John's undergraduate career was spent laboriously in the attempt to fit himself for the position of a sanitary engineer. Taking his unusual knowledge on subjects closely connected with this branch of study as a basis, we feel assured in saying that should he continue to increase his practical skill after leaving college at the same rate as he gained detail knowledge on the said subject during his senior year, he will soon be an authority. His classroom lectures, which attracted the attention of students in other branches of engineering, are sufficient proof of his ability to express himself clearly and his real understanding of the study. John is a typical agricultural student, and while his person presents no vegetation scene, he is from the country and proud of it. Holidays and vacation time cannot come too frequently for John and no time is lost in making a quick return to the old homestead. While we have no proof of the statement we feel safe in saying that his home-coming is no little event in Carroll County. He is a very quiet individual and cannot be accused of being an annoyance, as is a true rating of many of his fellow students, especially when by chance some one may strangely be infused with the energy to do a little work.

name: Rosenau, William Hellman
motto: "A college joke to cure the dumps." —Swift.
clubs: Class Basketball and Lacrosse Teams (1918) F., So., Entered Class in Senior Year
Assistant Manager of 1916 Lacrosse and Football Teams
description: Willie Rosenau, the helping hand to the football manager, though to hear the coach we might be led to suppose otherwise, from the day that he entered the University has been the cause of many a laugh and the object of much good-natured teasing. His short abrupt almost terse way of expressing himself, with that "baby stare" from behind his large glasses make him a highly entertaining person to carry on a conversation with. What Willie does with himself after classes none but a very chosen few seem to have any idea. We know that he hopes to some day in the dim future become an M.D., and we hope, for his sake, that his aspirations in this line will prove more successful than his role of would-be-manager. At one time he intended to be manager of at least three activities. The only part of Willie's make-up we can't quite understand is why he should be so unaccountably devoted to consuming fountain pens. We fear that unless he can control this habit it will grow upon him and be of serious detriment to his instruments when he gets that M.D.

name: Scarborough, Harold Ellicott
motto: "For I am nothing, if not critical." —Othello.
description: This young man, small of stature but mighty in brains, was a leader and organizer in the great revolt against the despotism of the (in)famous Whitney (nee Ruggles) in 1916. All the evidence we have been able to collect concerning him indicates that he is a satirist of no mean ability, being held in great fear by many of the professors, and consequently receiving many 10's he would not otherwise have had marked up to his credit. Harold, in his early undergraduate life, had a decided aversion to the Y. M. C. A., but in his senior year he surprised his friends as well as the Y. M. C. A. secretary by voluntarily offering his dollar and enrolling as a member. Why this sudden change of mind no one seems to know. Those who have studied the situation most closely are inclined toward the idea that Dr. Slonimsky's ethical principles were a strong factor in bringing about the change. If so, we are deeply indebted to the Doctor for his part in Harold's reformation.

name: Schaeffer, Carl Lawyer
motto: "By silence I hear other men's imperfections and conceal my own." —Zeno.
description: He is one of those quiet but sociable individuals who help make life more pleasant, but do it in such a way that it takes some time to fully realize how good it was to have them around. Like most folks of that type he conversed without telling us much about himself and we have been much surprised lately to learn that in spite of the high cost of gasoline, his Overland may frequently be seen either going to or returning from Hanover, Pa. The cause of these visits seems explained by the arrival of scented envelopes, postmarked Hanover and addressed to Mr. Carl Schaeffer. He received an A. B. from Western Maryland College and seemed destined to be a pedagogue, but in view of the fact that he has studied engineering at Johns Hopkins we do not know whether his career along this line has been terminated or merely interrupted.

name: Silberstein, Eli
motto: "Comb down your hair; look, look! it stands upright." —Shakespeare.
description: Eli is the possessor of a voice which can easily be heard and recognized up to a distance of half a mile. Furthermore, he frequently works that voice at the limit of its capacity and seldom below its maximum intensity. He also has a habit of borrowing quantities of paper and other drawing materials, and has acquired a reputation for being careless about repaying these loans, unless someone can hook his card and buy the desired goods at Thompson's store. If we dare insinuate that Eli is sponging on us, he loudly and indignantly informs us that he has a whole carload at home but that day forgot to bring the material under discussion. However, in spite of these little oddities, we have been glad to have "Benesch" in our class. He is quite an elocutionist, appearing to the best advantage in selections from Kipling, particularly "Gunga Din" and "Oonts". While taking a course in "Design of Structures," he also originated for our amusement, "The Dance of the I-Beams," the counterpart and rival of Howard's great Egyptian dance. "Silber" has a habit of getting whatever he sets out after, and we do not hesitate to predict that he will be successful in life.

name: William Howser Skinner
motto: "I love it, I love it, and who shall dare to chide me for loving the old armchair?"
clubs: Assistant Manager Football and Track Teams, So.
First Lieutenant and Battalion Adjutant, R. O. T. C.
Secretary and Treasurer Cotillon Club, S.
description: If we should be asked to express an opinion of "Bill" based upon his attitude toward college activities, we should say that he was decidedly a broad-minded individual. For "Bill" has taken part in all the activities and has been prominent in several. Encased in a huge white sweater, William made a conspicuous as well as a capable first baseman on our class baseball team. But "Bill" is of rather generous dimensions and soon gave up such strenuous work for a position requiring less physical exertion. Consequently, in his third year, we hear of him being manager of the track team. This year he is chairman of the Cotillon Board and is the Battalion Adjutant of the R. O. T. C. His robust figure pretty well fills out that uniform and he looks more like an old campaigner than a member of the Reserve Corps. Altogether he is a pleasant, friendly chap and one who has done his share for the class.

name: Egbert Leroy Smith
motto: "I never, with important air, In conversation overbear. Gay—Fables."
clubs: Varsity Track Team, F., So., J., S.
Member Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
Private in R. O. T. C.
description: "Smitty" is a regular walking bureau of information. Or perhaps to be more exact, a bureau of correction. If there is any subject under the sun about which he doesn't know more than any other student in the University, that subject has not come before us for discussion during the past four years. No matter how good the authority we quote to substantiate our claims, Le Roy always knows better for he has tried it. However, on only one subject are we willing to admit his superiority, and that is music. Although his musical tastes descend at times to such low planes as "I've Got a Mule That Can" and "The Last Cigar," he can sing, and sing well, much more edifying selections. He has been one of the mainstays of the Glee and Instrumental Clubs since entering the University and to find a man as faithful and able will be a hard task. In the other lines of endeavor also, "Smitty" has taken an active part and has been a loyal and worthy member of the track team and 'seventeen.

name: Edmund Law Rogers Smith
motto: "The warrior who cultivates his mind polishes his arms." —*Boufflers.*
clubs: Assistant Business-Manager, Y. M. C. A. Handbook, 1916, 1917
Assistant Business Manager of the News-Letter
description: E. L. R. Smith was one of the charter members of the Spirical Society. This is quite an honor, and we must congratulate him not only for this but also for the worthy ends that the society accomplished. As far as we can learn these consisted of finding the most humorous way in which to tease the live stock kept in the Biology Laboratory and giving the S. P. C. A. grounds for a suit against the University. He is also Assistant Business Manager of the News-Letter, helped get up the Spring Program and won his numerals in football and Lacrosse. A very peculiar thing about Smith is his present figure. When Ed first came to Hopkins he was perfectly normal and almost perfectly thin. In his second year, however, he contracted pleurisy in a serious form and as a result his heart was moved to the right side of his body. Evidently this was a safe place for it, because Smitty immediately began to get fat, and now, instead of a worried thin man, we have the genial, rolly polly E. L. R. Smith, Senior. As a student he has few peers and his major subject, Latin, is easily conquered without loss of weight. He is also an authority on the Army and Navy and had ambitions to become a rear admiral in the Mosquito Fleet. He has changed his mind, however, and has enlisted as a major-general in Battery A.

name: Randolph Smith
motto: "To myself alone do I owe my fame." —Corneille.
clubs: Associate Editor News-Letter, F., So., J., S.
description: SMITH doesn't mean very much, but stick a Randolph in front of it and you have a synonym for popularity and aggressiveness. Randolph is a rather quiet fellow, who generally accomplishes, with a great deal of success, whatever he undertakes. He has shown much ability along literary lines, having served as associate editor for both the Hullabaloo and the News-Letter. Then, too, he has been a member of the debating council, and the success of both our class and Varsity teams has been largely due to his efforts. So far you might get the impression that Randolph is a very serious individual, but to have seen him as the worried assistant manager of our lacrosse team while a junior would have changed that impression. Trying to answer shouts for "Aw Randolph" from every direction at once (whether he was wanted or not), gave him quite the appearance of a mother hen trying to administer to an overgrown brood of chicks. But Randolph saw the joke, and in his senior year as Varsity manager arranged the finest schedule we probably ever had. The best we can wish him is that he may be as successful after college as he was at Hopkins.

name: Wesley Royston Smith
description: Royston Smith of ready smile and quick repartee. Royston is known to everybody as a rather quiet and thoroughly likeable person. He possesses the blessed gift of quietly dropping off to sleep when he is not interested in his surroundings; and, when in repose, he once made such an appealing picture that Dr. Hollander could not forbear requesting the remainder of the class not to disturb Royston's slumbers. The dance and the foaming seidel know him not: he takes his recreation by playing basketball and going to the theatre. When there is no good play in town, he will even go to the movies! Yea, it is said that he was one of the first to witness that popular film play "a la Kellerman" that appeared at a West Lexington Street theatre on one Thursday last spring. The upperclassmen will recall the excitement and the whispered rumors that passed through the student body at this time. It is said that Royston got advanced dope on the picture from a friend during Dr. Roulston's "deutsch" seminary. At any rate, Royston was seen at the movies promptly at 1.15 P. M., only a very short time after being excused from class.

name: Alexander Alan Steinbach
motto: "It is strange so great a statesman should be so sublime a poet." —Bulwer-Lytton.
clubs: Captain and Rebuttal Speaker Class Debating Team, J.
Winner Gold "H" and Blue "H" Varsity Debating, J., S.
Hopkins Representative Intercollegiate Peace Contest, J.
Rebuttal Speaker Class Debating Team, S.
description: It is strange so great a statesman should be so sublime a poet. Steinie possesses to an extraordinary degree the qualifications of a great scientist. His inexhaustible thirst for knowledge leads him to undergo an amount of exertion only possible for an Edison,—or a Steinbach. In biology especially, he displays an enthusiasm for research that is almost unbelievable. It may be safely stated that he can break more test tubes in the space of one laboratory period than any other person, living or dead, could have broken in an interval of time commensurate with that in which the Trojan war was waged. During lectures, he is all attention, leaning forward in his eagerness and interrupting the learned discourse with questions such as, "Does a chicken have teeth, and if so, is a frog's hop a fashionable affair?" There is only one thing in the world louder than Steinie's clothes, and that is his voice. When he is speaking—and that is eternally—it fills the corridors like the far-away sound of the sea: then, as the speech grows in vehemence, it storms the vestibules of our auditory organs until we must flee in order to escape with our lives. As in the case of artillerymen, there should be a rule providing that whenever he speaks, all those within hearing distance of him must open their mouths so as to relieve the shock of the intonation. But he is a good scout and quite a poet (see this book for verification of the latter statement) and we all wish him well.

name: Strobel, Peyton Brown
motto: "You must wake and call me early, Call me early, mother dear."
clubs: Runner-up in Spring Tennis Tournament So.
Varsity Tennis Team So., J., S.
Violin Soloist for Musical Clubs S.
Member Y. M. C. A. Cabinet J., S.
description: "Peyt" as a tennis player is to be rated as rather clever, while as a possessor of little or no pep and animation he is given an unusually close race by none other than Charles Webster Chesley. Both it must be admitted would rather sleep than attend a nine o'clock class, and Strobel gets the choice due to the fact that he, even after making the class, would still rather sleep through the lecture. He does, however, show an increment of interest at times and butts in with questions that are indeed over-ripe. Other than wielding a racquet on the courts, excluding his desire to sleep, Strobel is in his glory in roaming about the Senior M. E. draughting room and inspecting the accomplishments of his fellow students, presumably to determine exactly how much work he must do to catch up to date. One of the side-lines in which Peyt is really proficient is when playing the role of a sailor. One entire summer was spent in naval training, and as a result he is an authority on marine topics, to a somewhat greater extent than on engineering subjects. He must be given credit for getting through with the least amount of real work, and we bid him farewell with best wishes.

name: Straus, Henry Lobe
motto: "The reason firm, the temperate will, Endurance, foresight, strength and skill. Wordsworth."
clubs: Member Varsity Track and Relay Team, So., J., S.
Class Track and Relay Team, F., So., J., S.
description: The Big Swede; whose ambition is to be a farmer, who will probably be an engineer, and who ought to have been an insurance agent. Can you imagine the gallant captain of our track team, who dashes past the stands while the fair damsels seated therein cry, "Isn't he grand!" and who is the Editor of this book,—not to mention the man who KNEW that Dr. Gilpin couldn't make mesoalic acid,—can you imagine him digging potatoes? I am afraid that some morning he will be found out in the fields arguing with the turnips that they should have been up a week earlier. And isn't it a crying shame that a man who will argue himself blue in the face about anything at all, irrespective of whether he knows anything pertaining to the subject or not, should spend his life chasing B. T. U.'s? Since he has been a member of 1917, Harry has done many things of note, accomplishments too numerous for tabulation here, but we cannot help congratulating him upon his election to the Omicron Delta Kappa Society, his most recent honor.
