name: Stuart, Edward Merceron
motto:
"Work apace! apace! apace! apace!"
"Honest labour bears a lovely face."
clubs: Member of Varsity Lacrosse Team, F., So., J., S.
Assistant Manager Football Team, So., J.
Member of Athletic Board, So., J., S.
Manager of Class Lacrosse Team, F., So.
Hopkins Correspondent to The Baltimore News
description: What a task has fallen upon a fellow-classmate of Eddie Stuart's to write up this great engineer, scribe, and athlete of 1917. Eddie’s qualities as an engineer are well known to all his friends in that department. Prof. Christie one day revealed the secret of Eddie’s success to us, and since then the saying; “Christie was right,” has never faded from our memories. As a scribe, Merceron has no superior, excluding not even the illustrious Harold Scarborough. Give Merceron a few items of news and he will show you a column in the next issue of Baltimore’s best sporting paper. For the past three years Ed has been the goal tender on the lacrosse team and during that time scores for our opponents have been about as scarce as the proverbial hen’s teeth. When the opposing team swept up the field long will we remember the salutary effect upon our close defense men of his well-known battle-cry: “All tight here.” Certainly no one in the class was more deserving of the honor of being an ΟΔΚ man than Ed, and we shall always remember with pleasure that we were members of the same class.

name: Sutton, Paul Windom
motto: "Zealous, yet modest; innocent, though free; Patient of toil; serene amidst alarms; Inflexible in faith; invincible in arms." —Beattie, The Minstrel.
clubs: President of Y. M. C. A. S.
Captain of Varsity Lacrosse Team J.
Member of Class Track, Basketball, and Lacrosse Teams
Delegate to Athletic Association So., J.
description: Zealous, yet modest; innocent, though free; Patient of toil; serene amidst alarms; Inflexible in faith; invincible in arms. Eggie is what we call him for short, in spite of his unquestionable fear of a chicken under the popular present day rating. His classification, due to his versatility in athletic togs, is none other than the "Black and Blue Jim Thorpe" and his athletic ability has certainly measured up to the mark. Nailing a position on the Lacrosse team in his freshman year, Eggie has held down an attack job for four years and captained the team while a junior. During the indoor season he slacked his athletic thirst by turning his hand to track, as well as performing cleverly as a member of the class basketball team. As he is going to the Medical School, it is likely that he will be a valuable asset for the baseball team next year. Just where Eggie gets his athletic ability is not known, but we think we are right in saying that it is the nature of the beast, for each of the several members of the Sutton family who have come to Hopkins have carried away more than their share of "H's". As an undergraduate Eggie took interest in the various activities at Hopkins and finished up his Senior year as president of his class, as well as the Athletic Association and the Y. M. C. A. As a fellow student he has always given one the impression of a quiet and orderly individual, but certain tales that have been circulated about the campus regarding his attitude as a member of the English Literature class under Ma Green mark him as the bull in the china shop. Regardless of this failure, Eggie has been a valuable member of 1917. His real interest and valuable help in all worthy causes have made him an Omicron Delta Kappa man. Our memories and best wishes will follow him as he follows his aspirations for an M.D.

name: J. Trueman Thompson
motto: "I'll buy a bit of land and make a start, I'll build a little homestead, and I'll win a little wife, And raise ten little kids to cheer my heart."
clubs: Class President, F., H. A. A. (Track) So.
Varsity Track Team, F., So., J.
Class Track Team, F., So., Class Numerals, F., So.
description: Tommie is one of the live wires in our class. If there should be any doubt of this, touch him unexpectedly and watch him scatter whatever he has in his hands all over the room or pound the breath out of whoever is nearest him. Tommy was elected our first class president, and that we made no mistake in the selection is attested by the fact that since then whenever we have needed a leader in our various activities, he has usually been chosen for the position. The same qualifications led to his election to the OΔK Society. As a student he is one of the best in the class, and will sit up as late as may be necessary to overcome difficulties which present themselves in his courses. According to one of his intimates, Trueman would not have sat up so late as he generally does if he did not spend so much of the evening in West Arlington. Nevertheless we shall all remember with pleasure our association with Tommy and be glad that we were his classmates.

name: Twigg, John Milton
motto: "Little dew drops of Celestial melody." —Carlyle.
description: Milton is a sturdy branch of the famous Twigg family that spreads for the most part over a section of Maryland known as Twiggville. Whether the return of this brilliant member to his section of the woods will necessitate a change in name to Treetown is a question worthy of consideration. Living up to the reputation of the other Twiggs as clever football artists, Milton himself came to Hopkins with a long pedigree which classed him as somewhat of a devil in gridiron togs. Modesty, which his features portray, is thought to have prevented his taking up the game in earnest under the Black and Blue colors. Milton has gone through Hopkins as a side-kick to Young, having even gone so far as to exercise a paternal influence over Mac when studies were not a sufficient incentive to burn the midnight oil. This pair, after frequent attempts to spread joy while freshmen, proved quite successful, and in a conservative manner continued to cut up and take in movies even through their senior year. Milton is a quiet and conscientious worker, and possesses that valuable asset of sticktoitiveness which will produce results in his life work. This quality was not developed until he finally passed off Mathematics 2. Just how many years he labored over this perplexing subject is not recalled, but he celebrated the accomplishment of the requirements under Dr. Hulburt long before some other members of the class who started with him.

name: Jesse Myron Upperco
motto: "Blessed is the man who, having nothing to say, abstains from giving us wordy evidence of the fact." —George Eliot.
description: Jesse is the authority of our class on agriculture, and living in the wilds of Maryland, has had practical experience to support his arguments on what caused potatoes to sell for such an exorbitant price in his senior year. While these views do not agree with the transportation theory as advanced by Dr. Barnett, he stubbornly maintains his point. Together with all but one of the other members of the mechanical engineers, however, he joins in accord in voicing his opinion that "Christie was right." Yes, Jesse chose the field of mechanical engineering as his vocation, and should he continue to show the same improvement throughout his whole career as an engineer as was exhibited during his four years at Hopkins, he will soon be at the top of the ladder. He surprised even his closest friends, during the mid-years of his last year, by pulling 10 flat in most of his exams. It was as much of a surprise to all of us, because he cracks his books only as a last resort to keep from wasting time. Jesse figures that it is much more enjoyable to read a good story, or to listen to one of Darley's jokes than to chase B. T. U.s through a textbook, and few of his fellow-students will argue with him. With his reserved manner and real ability we launch him into his work with our best wishes, feeling assured of his success.

name: Wacker, Herman
motto: "My hands are clean, but my heart has somewhat of impurity." —Euripides.
description: As a freshman Herman bore the reputation of being an earnest worker and quite timid. But as he grew older and reached the more mature age of a senior, he seems to have grown away from these early characteristics. The timid freshman has developed into a real Beau Brummel, and we hear that the course in Mechanical Engineering he is supposedly pursuing occupies a very small part of his time. Coupling these facts and his association with Reiner, we are led to believe that there must be a woman in the case. We have often seen him wearing a worried expression, but whether this has any connection with the foregoing facts we are unable to say. Wacker, like his friend Cohen, talks but little, and only a chosen few enjoy his confidence. Hence our lack of knowledge regarding him.

name: John Stuart Watson
motto: "Men possessed with an idea cannot be reasoned with." —Froude.
clubs: Member Engineering Society and Student Branch A. S. M. E.
description: Now, when I was out on the Sue Canal, or Kansas City, or Denver, or any other place in America, for Watts has been to them all. No, you can't mention a place he hasn't been, a thing he hasn't seen or a thing he hasn't done. Whether stalking Jack Rabbits on the vast western plains or shooting through the Grand Canyon of the Colorado on a raft; whether gliding over the frozen bosom of Lake Huron or making thiophenolsulphonic acid, Watts is there, and if you don't believe it LOOK AT HIM! But you can't appreciate it without seeing the original—for Stuart has traveled extensively, as has been stated above, and the tales of his experiences have kept us amused since Freshman Year. As a Freshman, he came out for two spring teams, but trouble with his eyes kept him from indulging too strenuously, and since then he has become more sot in his ways (he hails from Charles County, Gawd bless you) and has taken to smoking Pall Mall Cigarettes, less study and corpulence. We all like Watso,—why shouldn't we, he's a good old scout, and when we hold our class reunion twenty years from now, we expect to find him at the very top of the ladder—and still going up.

name: Webb, William David
motto: "Night after night / He sat and bleared his eyes with books." — Longfellow.
description: Few of the aspirants for the title of Civil Engineer in our class, not excluding Guy Bryan and his associates, have proved themselves so diligent during class hours as our friend Bill. Living in the wilds of the country, where the sight of a train is an unusual occurrence, and compelled to take the early accommodation for Baltimore, William D. is one of the first to break the morning stillness at Homewood. Incidentally, it is quite interesting to hear Webb tell of how one morning when on his way to the University he found Fritz Wood returning home from one of his extra special engagements of the previous evening; but this we will omit, since he seems quite certain that Fritz was sober. Webb, together with Noble Owings, is one of the refining influences in the Senior C. E. Department. Being of a quiet and retiring disposition when not aroused, his capacity for work is the most noticeable thing about him. He seems never to tire of laying out theoretical sewerage systems, and shines brightly at bridge drill, and in making cement briquetes. All in all, he is an ambitious student and gives promise of being a comer. He should be a great credit to Doc Tilden after he grabs that imitation sheepskin in June, provided, of course, that we don't have war.

name: Weech, Alexander Ashley
motto: "He was a scholar, and a ripe and good one." —Shakespeare.
description: Enough said! Ashley is a minister's son and all that goes with it. While not really a bad boy, he is ready to go on a lark or play a prank any time; as ready as the rest of us, except, of course, Trueman Thompson, who takes the lead in that respect. Some men go through the University in three years, but here is the first one who finished before he knew it. Ashley was so near graduation at the end of his third year that he had to take only a course or two at the summer school to make up the required credits. So while we were working hard during the dreary winter days, he was pursuing the policy of watchful waiting for his diploma; also he held a position as chemist for a firm down at Curtis Bay. Weech was, and still is, an ardent supporter of the track team; he used to come out and jog along with the others, and now his voice can be heard encouraging those who are running the race for Hopkins. Another place where his voice is heard is with the Glee Club, where he sang faithfully for three years. Those who are acquainted with Ashley know that his cheerful manner and hearty laugh serve to brighten up many an otherwise stiff and formal occasion.

name: Weeks, Walter Evans
motto: "To shun all lures that debauch the soul, The orgied rites of the rich."
clubs: S. A. I. A. A. Champion in One-Mile and Two-Mile Events, S.
Holder of Hopkins Two-Mile Record, S.
Member Student Branch A. S. M. E., S.
description: He is so pure that he makes an "ivory soap ad" blush. And we are proud of him. And besides being a pillar of the church he is also a couple of pillars to the Track Team, being South Atlantic Champion in both the one and two-mile runs, holding the Hopkins record in the latter event. He is the youngest of the Senior Engineers, but not, by far, the dullest. He has the happy faculty of finishing well whatever he starts, unless it's an argument on Prohibition, and is bound to make a success of life. Our association with him has always been pleasant, except when he was collecting for Power, but we forgive him for that, and wish him the top of the ladder.

name: Fancis Howard Wilson
motto: "He is a soldier fit to stand by Caesar And give directions." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Lacrosse Squad, F., So., J., S.
description: Howard—generally known as "Ard," is a hard worker, both in his studies and in his work for the University. Though never a shining light, he has always kept well out of the danger zone, and never yet has his name appeared on that fatal sombre list. At the outset "Father Bill" recognized latent possibilities in "Ard" as a lacrosse player, and almost any afternoon in the cage one could hear him telling "Ard" to make a little noise and let the rest know that he was there. He has. After two years' trying he successfully made the lacrosse team, and will, undoubtedly, play again at his old position his last year. As has often been held up to us—it is the quiet, persistent worker that gets there every time—and that is just what "Ard" is. He has never been known to make much noise—except on the lacrosse field—and his stick-to-itness has won a place for him in the hearts of us all.

name: Seager, Bertram
motto: "Above the pitch, out of tune and off the hinges." —Rabelais.
description: Like St. Patrick is said to have been, is a great little man; only his greatness does not lie in snake charming, but in man charming. What he can't do with a mandolin isn't worth doing. He can play twins and triplets and sextettes and things in a way that sets your heart going pit-a-pat. Small wonder that he leads the Mandolin Club! Like all great men, "Winny" is very busy, but he has a little spare time, and his favorite occupation on such rare occasions is to perfect oil mixers for Dr. Thomas, and plot curves for Young, as well as attend to the numerous duties of vice-president of the Hullabalectric Society. If there ever existed a person with a golden disposition, it is our young hero. He takes such great care not to force his company on anyone that he usually comes into the room after the lecture has begun. He cannot bear to overwork anybody, not even himself, and that is probably why he fails to tickle himself under the chin some mornings when such tickling would be to decided advantage. And when "Micky" Reiner springs a bum joke, even though there is nothing funny in it, "Winny's" melodious laughter ripples forth into the silence of Room 117 in a manner that would compliment Mark Twain or Charlie Chaplin.

name: Wood, Walter Alexander
motto: "I am not a one-woman man." —Fair and Warmer.
clubs: Member Class Baseball Team, F., So., J., S.
description: The above quotation is a perfect description of one member of our class, "Fritz" Wood, to be exact. For to think of "Fritz" is to think of seeing him with some fair damsel. In his mind he neatly classified all kinds: wild, cultivated, garden, and hothouse varieties. He is a combination of Beau Brummel, Don Juan and Lord Chesterfield. "Fritz" is never so happy as when gliding over some polished floor to the strains of an orchestra, with his arm about one of his "extra specials." Aside from the question of girls, he is a very normal young man. He never fails in any of his courses, neither does he get 10's; he never studies too hard, nor does he fall behind in his work. He talks fluently and entertainingly on all subjects but one, and that—women. Whenever that mystic word is uttered, "Fritz" becomes strangely silent, and dreams of his next big date. He has few student activities, although he does contribute an occasional sketch to THE HULLABALOO. All through the morning he thinks longingly of the lunch hour, and when it arrives, rushes to the soda fountain, where some unfortunate has to buy him a chocolate malted milk. Not that "Fritz" bums any drinks, but he has an uncanny habit of being able to match any coin that is laid on the counter, be it heads or tails, or both. His thirst quenched, he can draw for hours without a murmur.

name: Louis McComas Young
motto: "My Christian Friends, dis story proves dat eben men is human— He'd had a dozen fancy coats, ef he'd 'a been a 'ooman!"
clubs: Member of Engineering Society, J., S.
description: Mac's career as a phenomenal student began back in the old days when he showed signs of unusual intellect at Washington County High School, where he prepared for Hopkins. It began then and flourished surprisingly, but, strange to say, in spite of the fact that his scholastic accomplishments approach the stage of brilliancy they never quite reach it. It is true that he hovers about a mark far above the head of the average student, but too many dances in one week at—well, a certain dance hall—invariably halt his deserving climb. The reader should have been informed before that Young is an electrical engineer, but this might have been concluded, for he is so filled with electric phenomena that his brain generates the said juice, and his head always has that characteristic porcupine effect. Aside from electricity, Young has been strangely attracted to the study of by-product recovery of coal, or rather is intensely interested in the laboratory for this subject. The experience of such work is one of the many things that go to make a man perfect, and other students who have observed his frequent visits envy his impulsive but wise selection. Track claimed Mac's attention during the freshman year, but when a rumor circulated that several of the correspondents to Vogue, having heard of his unique appearance in his abbreviated uniform, were on his trail, he refused to be the center of universal amusement, and bade farewell to the spikes.
